did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models (specifically DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, InceptionV3, MobileNet variants, ResNet variants, and VGG16) to evaluate skin disease diagnosis using the HAM10000 dataset. The results include classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for identifying skin cancer types.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results evaluating state-of-the-art deep learning models (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, etc.) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section, experimental results with performance metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score) in Table II, and concludes that EfficientNetV2B0 is the top performer. Although published in a workshop (IWIS), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Modern Deep Learning Models for Skin Disease Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study evaluates the effectiveness of modern deep learning models for skin disease diagnosis using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers assessed state-of-the-art architectures, including DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, InceptionV3, MobileNet variants, ResNet variants, and VGG16. To address class imbalance, the training data was oversampled, and the dataset was split into 80% training and 20% testing sets. The models were evaluated based on precision, recall, accuracy, and F1-score metrics. The results indicate that EfficientNetV2B0 achieved the highest performance across all metrics, followed closely by EfficientNetB0. The authors conclude that while EfficientNetV2B0 is the top performer, EfficientNetB0 offers a better balance of performance and computational efficiency for practical clinical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class in the HAM10000 dataset used for skin cancer diagnosis classification. Original train data: 5364 images.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease diagnostic categories (7 classes: Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The study evaluates deep learning models (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, InceptionV3, MobileNet variants, ResNet variants, VGG16) for diagnosing skin cancer types. Performance is measured using Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and F1-score metrics (Table II). The task involves assigning discrete diagnostic labels to dermoscopy images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, providing clinical metadata including lesion attributes, patient information, and diagnostic annotations provided by dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetV2B0, InceptionV3, MobileNet variants, ResNet variants, VGG16) with oversampling applied to address class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of model performance metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score).
use.other: Models were pre-trained on ImageNet before being fine-tuned on this dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares model performance using descriptive metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score) without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNetV2B0 achieved the highest performance across all metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score). EfficientNetB0 was the second-ranked model. The performance differentials between the top-ranked models were modest, within the range of approximately 5% to 8%. EfficientNetV2B0 is approximately 1.5 times larger in size compared to EfficientNetB0.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetV2B0, EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, MobileNet, MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3Large, MobileNetV3Small, ResNet50, ResNet50V2, VGG16

architecture_details.notes: The last layer of the original pre-trained models was removed and replaced with the custom head sequence to accommodate the 7 classes of the HAM10000 dataset.

training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Oversampling of training data for underrepresented classes

preprocessing_techniques: Oversampling of training data for underrepresented classes to address class imbalance
Dataset split into 80% training and 20% testing sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 training dataset. Underrepresented classes were oversampled to ensure a similar number of images across all classes (e.g., Melanoma increased from 890 to 5990, Dermatofibroma from 92 to 5570).

conclusions: EfficientNetV2B0 achieved the highest performance across all evaluated metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and F1-score) among the tested modern deep learning models on the HAM10000 dataset.

EfficientNetB0 ranked second in performance but is considered a prudent choice for practical, real-world clinical applications due to its better balance of computational efficiency and model size compared to EfficientNetV2B0.
The performance differentials between the top-ranked models (EfficientNetV2B0, EfficientNetB0, and DenseNet121) are modest, ranging approximately between 5% and 8%.
EfficientNetV2B0 is approximately 1.5 times larger in size than EfficientNetB0, which contributes to the trade-off analysis favoring EfficientNetB0 for resource-constrained environments.
Oversampling was necessary to address significant class imbalance in the HAM10000 training dataset, particularly for underrepresented classes such as Dermatofibroma (df) and Vascular lesions (vasc).
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is multi-source and may introduce population or dataset bias; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.

Significant class imbalance in the original training data was addressed via oversampling (e.g., Dermatofibroma increased from 92 to 5570 images), which may artificially inflate performance metrics for minority classes and does not reflect real-world prevalence.
The dataset was split into 80% training and 20% testing sets with no separate validation set reported, raising potential concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the test set.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was conducted to compare model performances; the authors note performance differentials between top models are modest (5-8%).
The paper is published in a workshop (IWIS) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may limit the depth of critical review and generalizability of findings.

While EfficientNetV2B0 achieved the highest accuracy (91.41%), the authors argue EfficientNetB0 is more suitable for clinical deployment due to its lower computational cost (FLOPS, latency) and smaller model size, highlighting a trade-off between peak performance and practical efficiency.
Models were pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned; the paper does not report results for models trained from scratch, limiting the assessment of transfer learning's specific contribution.
